summary: VC-funded startups oversimplify revenue into 'bad' services and 'good' recurring product revenue, but reality is more nuanced. Rocklin breaks down five revenue types—consulting, funded development, support, hard-to-deploy products, and cloud SaaS—each with different tradeoffs in scalability, dependability, and product development value. Consulting and funded development, while not scalable, provide invaluable customer insight early on. Support contracts are undervalued despite being genuinely recurring revenue. Cloud SaaS is the VC ideal but requires convincing customers your systems are secure, which varies dramatically by sector.
key_points: The binary framing of 'services bad, product good' oversimplifies how different revenue types contribute to building a company
Consulting services are actually great when starting out because there's no better way to understand customer pain points than working closely with paying customers
Support contracts are genuinely annually recurring revenue, yet founders often have to convince VCs to count them as ARR
Hard-to-deploy products still count as 'real' product revenue but carry hidden costs like supporting old versions and working within customer environments you can't directly access
Cloud SaaS is high-velocity once working, but getting there requires solving the trust problem—easy with SMBs, hard with enterprise, finance, and government
money_quotes: VC funded startups are weird. They (rightly) care more about valuation than about revenue.
There's no better way to understand customer pain points than figuring out what they're willing to pay for and working closely with them.
One needs to be careful that we're learning from this experience, rather than just chasing money (although that's ok too if you're doing it intentionally).
The beautiful thing here is that if your software works perfectly then this is free to provide. Your software doesn't work perfectly, so this isn't free.
I typically have to convince business people that this is ARR, despite the fact that it's definitely annually recurring revenue.
Cloud SaaS is considered to be the highest velocity approach because once it works it's easy to scale out to lots of customers with low effort. Of course, it may be really hard to get to that point.
Rather than say services are bad and product is good we should be asking questions about how scalable, dependable, high margin, and in line with your product development they are.
key_insight: Revenue quality exists on a spectrum of scalability, dependability, and product alignment—founders should evaluate each type on these dimensions rather than accepting the oversimplified 'services bad, product good' framing.
